title: What in the dark do I think I have to guard against?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for examining what night-time vigilance is actually protecting against.
excerpt: A journal prompt set from Day 12 of the SLEEP program. The prompts ask what the body is bracing for at bedtime, not the generic "I can't sleep" but the specific, named thing the vigilance thinks it is guarding against.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 12 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795), "Release Fear of the Night."

content.text: A journal prompt set from Day 12 of the SLEEP program. The prompts go under the surface of "I can't sleep" to ask what the body's nighttime vigilance is actually protecting against. Twenty minutes, no editing.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
What does my body actually do as bedtime approaches?
Physical description: shoulders, jaw, stomach, breath. What changes in the late afternoon or evening?
When did I first start being afraid of bedtime, or of not sleeping?
Try to locate a period or a specific memory. What else was happening then?
What does my body think it is guarding against in the dark?
Name it as specifically as you can. A person, a situation, a feeling, a memory. What is the vigilance protecting you from?
Is that thing still an actual present danger, or is the body running an old programme?
Write the answer directly. If it's an old programme, write when it was installed and what it cost to learn it.
What would the body need to believe in order to feel safe enough to let the guard down at night?
Not a plan, a felt sense. What would have to be true about your situation, your body, your safety, for you to put the guard down?
Adapted from Day 12 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
